schools.summary: Ellicott and Clarke distinguish the two effects of Abner's death: the king's hands grow feeble, the people are troubled because their negotiations with David are thrown into confusion.
Matthew Henry: Saul's house grows weaker; Ishbosheth had no strength but Abner's, and irresolution in a crisis forfeits both crown and life.
Gill identifies 'Saul's son' as Ishbosheth and notes Abner was murdered by Joab in Hebron, David's own city.
The note on Beeroth (a Gibeonite city reckoned to Benjamin) is read as underscoring the horror that the murderers were of Saul's own tribe; the past tense betrays the town was later deserted.
Clarke reads 'captains of bands' as captains of banditti/marauders, men well qualified for the bloody work they later did.
Gill takes them as captains of troops or of Ishbosheth's own guard, which aggravates the treachery.
Gittaim (cf. Neh. 11:33) cannot be firmly located; the flight was probably from Philistine incursions, and 'until this day' shows the writer stood near the events.
Gill dates the flight to the collapse after Saul's death, when border towns near the Philistines were abandoned.
The note on the lame Mephibosheth is inserted to show the house of Saul was practically extinct, no fit heir remaining; he is the Meribbaal of Chronicles.
Wesley: the low state of Saul's house was what emboldened the murderers, since only a lame child would be left; Clarke laments Jonathan's misfortune in his progeny.
Gill notes Jonathan, Saul's eldest, died with his father, leaving this crippled grandson.
The brothers reach Ishbosheth's house at Mahanaim during the customary midday siesta; his bed lay in the coolest, most retired part, leaving him exposed.
Matthew Henry reads the noon sleep as sloth: a feeble king who loved ease became easy prey; love not sleep lest ruin come.
Gill names the sons of Rimmon, Rechab and Baanah, at the door.
Under pretext of fetching wheat the brothers reached the king and smote him in the abdomen ('under the fifth rib', as Abner smote Asahel); the LXX adds a sleeping portress to explain their entry.
Wesley explains the wheat was stored in the royal granary and drawn by captains to pay soldiers in corn, so the errand raised no suspicion.
Gill supposes the guards asleep, off duty, or deceived by the familiar sight of officers fetching grain.
The repetition is normal Hebrew narrative, resuming the thread to add a new fact (the beheading); they carried the head all night down the Arabah to Hebron.
Gill reports Abarbinel's view that the brothers entered twice — first wounding, then returning to behead — to explain the repeated account.
The murderers claim no divine warrant but say providence avenged David, framing it to win his gratitude; his reply will invert their hope, as with the Amalekite at Ziklag.
Matthew Henry: a man may gain by a death yet abhor the means; David, trusting God to finish his own work, will not be beholden to murderers.
Gill and Clarke: like the lying Amalekite, the pair thought the head an acceptable gift by which to ingratiate themselves.
Matthew Henry: David, as avenger of blood, condemns the self-confessed murderers and swears by the God who redeemed his soul, refusing to reach the throne by unlawful means.
Clarke: David's life had often been sought, yet God always redeemed it; that providence now bids him punish men of blood, not reward them.
Gill: David answered the sons of Rimmon in a manner they did not expect.

themes: 'redeemed my soul out of all adversity'
trust that God will finish his work

schools.summary: The precedent of the Amalekite argues from lesser to greater; Ellicott reads 'which was the reward I gave him,' and Clarke notes euangelia = the messenger's reward, the root of 'gospel'.
Gill, with Abarbinel, infers the Amalekite had not truly killed Saul; David slew him for boasting himself the herald of a king's death.
'Righteous' means not at fault toward the assassins; to 'take you from the earth' is to purge the murderers' guilt from the land, which murder defiles until expiated (Deut. 19:13; Num. 35:33).
Clarke stacks the aggravations — an innocent man, in his own house, on his bed, killed by officers who owned him as king — the most abandoned treachery.
Gill: Ishbosheth was righteous relative to his murderers, his opposition to David springing from ignorance, not malice.
The murderers are mutilated (the hands that killed, the feet that ran for reward) and hung publicly by the pool as a warning, while Ishbosheth's head is honourably buried in Abner's tomb.
Clarke: the execution proved David a decided enemy to the destruction of Saul's house, showing the strictest regard for justice.
Matthew Henry warns that those who serve the Son of David by blood and fraud will find Christianity was never meant to destroy humanity.

themes: David clears his throne of blood
